sections.label: Situation Overview
sections.title: US-Iran ceasefire takes effect amid ongoing tensions
paragraphs: The United States and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire on Tuesday evening, hours before President Trump's deadline for Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz or face massive attacks on civilian infrastructure. Under the deal, Iran has agreed to ensure safe passage through the strategic waterway for coordination with Iranian armed forces, while Trump suspended his threat to destroy Iran's power plants and other infrastructure.

The fragile truce faced immediate challenges with Israel launching devastating attacks on Lebanon killing at least 254 people, claiming the ceasefire does not cover Hezbollah operations. Iranian officials reported oil traffic through the Strait of Hormuz was halted again hours after initial tanker movements, citing Israel's Lebanon strikes. Pakistan has invited US and Iranian delegations to Islamabad for negotiations beginning Friday.
key_facts: Two-week ceasefire agreed with Iran reopening Strait of Hormuz
Israeli strikes on Lebanon killed 254, wounded 1,165 on Wednesday
Negotiations scheduled in Islamabad from Friday April 10
Iran traffic restrictions resumed after Israeli Lebanon strikes
sections.australia_relevance: Australia's fuel security remains under pressure despite the ceasefire, with diesel prices hitting record highs and petrol still 33% above pre-war levels.

sections.label: Background
sections.title: 40-day conflict between US-Israel and Iran
paragraphs: The war began on February 28 when the US and Israel launched airstrikes across Iran, assassinating Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and inflicting civilian casualties. Iran retaliated with missile strikes against Israel and US bases while closing the Strait of Hormuz, through which 20% of global oil flows. The conflict escalated from long-standing tensions over Iran's nuclear program and its support for regional militias.

Death tolls remain disputed, with US-based rights group HRANA estimating almost 3,400 killed including over 1,600 civilians, while 13 US service members have died. Iran has maintained an internet blackout for 40 days, described as 936 hours of near-total disconnection from the outside world. The war has triggered the largest supply disruption in global oil market history according to the International Energy Agency.
key_facts: War began February 28 with US-Israeli strikes killing Iran's Supreme Leader
Strait of Hormuz closure disrupted 20% of global oil supplies
Nearly 3,400 killed including 1,600+ civilians (disputed figures)
40-day internet blackout continues across Iran

sections.label: Diplomacy
sections.title: Mixed signals on ceasefire scope and compliance
paragraphs: Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif mediated the ceasefire talks and claimed the deal covers 'Lebanon and elsewhere', but Israel's Netanyahu explicitly rejected including Lebanon operations. Iran presented a 10-point peace proposal that Trump called a workable basis for negotiation, reportedly demanding sanctions lifting, US troop withdrawals from regional bases, and compensation. Both sides are claiming victory, with Iranian officials saying the war is ending on Iran's terms.

Trump suggested China helped bring Iran to negotiations and indicated Vice President JD Vance might participate in Pakistan talks, though security concerns remain. Pakistan has already reported ceasefire violations in several areas, undermining the peace process. Qatar intercepted Iranian missile and drone attacks despite the ceasefire agreement, highlighting ongoing regional tensions.
key_facts: Pakistan mediating talks starting Friday in Islamabad
Iran's 10-point plan includes sanctions lifting and US withdrawals
Israel explicitly excludes Lebanon from ceasefire scope
Both Iran and US claiming victory from the agreement
sections.australia_relevance: Australia was among countries Trump criticised for not helping with the Iran war, alongside Japan, South Korea and NATO allies.

sections.label: Economy & Energy
sections.title: Australian fuel costs remain elevated despite ceasefire hopes
paragraphs: Australian petrol prices fell for the first time since the war began, dropping 5% to A$2.40 per litre in the week to Sunday, but remain about 33% higher than pre-conflict levels. Diesel hit record highs at A$3.13 per litre, the highest since records began in 2006. Economists warn the conflict could subtract up to 0.7 percentage points from GDP growth while adding up to 3 percentage points to inflation in worst-case scenarios.

The war has created what analysts call the largest supply disruption in global oil market history, with Brent crude prices surging above $100 per barrel. Australia's strategic fuel reserves remain non-compliant with International Energy Agency requirements, holding only 36 days of petrol, 34 days of diesel and 32 days of jet fuel - potentially covering just 26, 25 and 20 days respectively if critical services take priority during emergencies.
key_facts: Australian petrol down 5% to A$2.40/L but still 33% above pre-war
Diesel at record A$3.13/L, highest since 2006 records began
Australia holds only 26-36 days emergency fuel reserves
Potential 0.7% GDP loss and 3% inflation spike in worst case
sections.australia_relevance: Australia imports 90% of its liquid fuel, making it highly vulnerable to Middle East supply disruptions despite having signed energy trade agreements with Singapore.

sections.label: What to Watch
sections.title: Fragile ceasefire faces multiple pressure points
paragraphs: The two-week ceasefire window until April 22 will test whether diplomatic progress in Islamabad can produce a lasting agreement or whether tensions will reignite. Israel's continued Lebanon operations despite Pakistani mediation claims create immediate friction, with over 250 Lebanese killed since the ceasefire announcement. Iran has warned that any deviation from the agreement could trigger renewed violence, stating hands remain on the trigger.

Oil markets will closely watch whether the Strait of Hormuz remains fully operational or faces renewed restrictions, particularly given Iranian statements linking transit access to Israeli actions in Lebanon. The upcoming Reserve Bank meeting will signal how Australian policymakers respond to energy-driven inflation pressures, while Treasury modeling suggests prolonged disruption could push the economy toward recession with quarterly GDP contractions.
key_facts: Islamabad negotiations begin Friday April 10 for permanent deal
Israel-Lebanon fighting threatens ceasefire stability
Strait of Hormuz reopening remains conditional and uncertain
Australian recession risks rise if oil disruptions continue
sections.australia_relevance: The Reserve Bank's next meeting will be closely watched for policy responses to Iran war-driven inflation and fuel cost pressures on Australian households.
